This approach seems quite messy and likely to break under many circumstances. Firstly, you are using floating point values as dictionary keys, so I wonder how often you will encounter cases where the corresponding coefficients are not found because of floating point inequalities. Secondly, is there any reason that intermediate periods cannot be supported by interpolating between the nearest two periods? If not then I would suggest to add a function to return the coefficients for the periods immediately above and below imt_aux and then apply log-T linear-coefficient interpolation for each coefficient by looping over the keys of the dictionary.

```python

```
This will also be compatible with whatever arguments are added in the future.

Actually, is the signature of _custom_start_client and _orig_start_client seem to be the same. Then I suppose it should be
```python
def _custom_start_client(self, *args, **kwargs):
      self.banner_timeout = 45
      self._orig_start_client(*args, **kwargs)		 
```

My idea was that when there is an exception in the command there will be a traceback and exit code `1` (standard python) but when the upgrade fails server-side we have a distinct exit code `3` so that we can assert it in scripts and distinguish upgrade failures and runtime errors (e.g. server not running). Asserting both is then just an `exitcode != 0`.

I think exit code `2` should not be used since it is used by bash, see [Exit Codes With Special Meanings](http://tldp.org/LDP/abs/html/exitcodes.html) for example.

I'm not sure though if this makes a lot sense, what do you think?

```python

response = app.get('/')
```

I suspect the readability of this stretch of code would improve considerably if you were to make use of the `CelestialCoord.deproject` function.  I didn't trace through the math precisely, but I believe what you have done here is equivalent to:
```
u = sca_xc_tp_f * cos_pa - sca_yc_tp_f * sin_pa  # I might have the sign of sin_pa wrong here...
v = sca_xc_tp_f * sin_pa + sca_yc_tp_f * cos_pa
crval = pos.deproject(galsim.PositionD(u,v), projection='gnomonic')
header['CRVAL1'] = crval.ra / galsim.degrees
header['CRVAL2'] = crval.dec / galsim.degrees
```
I didn't test it out, but I think that's at least close to equivalent.  And much easier to follow.

Thankfully, this whole method is guarded by a lock (`with self._closing:`) which forces all calls to this to be handled serially regardless of concurrency. Mixing PCT encoded and decoded data can make a mess easy.
I believe we should follow the strategy:
1. Convert all user provided *path*, *prefix* etc into *normalized* (PCT-encoded actually) form by using `yarl`. Already normalized paths passed *as-is* by yarl.
2. Normalize given HTTP path (we do it).
3. Match normalized path to already pre-normalized patterns.
4. Return `request.match_info['name']` in **PCT-decoded** form because users most likely want getting `이름` instead of `'%EC%9D%B4%EB%A6%84`

If aiohttp code violates the rule somewhere -- it should be fixed.

Modularity should work both ways, when it makes sense. Specifically, modularity should respect layering: it should always be possible to use lower-level, more "base" components without higher-level components, but it may be acceptable to have higher-level components rely on base components. 

As an example, DataSet should be usable stand-alone, but Loop can require DataSet if that makes sense. An important corollary is that anything else that uses DataSet, like plotting, should work just as well if the DataSet is filled in by hand as when it is filled in using Loop.

Good point, I had just assumed they were thread safe but should have checked. Unfortunately locking ```self.provider``` would defeat the purpose of this PR. Methods of ```self.provider``` are called several times by ```StandardAutoscaler._update()``` , even if no nodes are launched/terminated, so we would effectively still be blocking on instance creation.

Note the only concurrent calls that can take place are one or more calls to create_node and at most one to another function, so the node providers do not need to be fully thread safe. In particular, both ```AWSNodeProvider``` and ```GCPNodeProvider``` are doing some internal caching right now which would be a little messy to make thread safe, but this is _not_ touched by ```create_node``` so does not need to be changed.

I think the main issue for both ```AWSNodeProvider``` and ```GCPNodeProvider``` is that ```boto3``` and ```googleclientapi``` are built on top of ```httplib``` which is not thread safe. The solution in both cases is to create thread-local sessions. Can @ericl and @hartikainen who are more familiar with these node providers than me comment on if there are any other problems with threading?

I'm a little unhappy with what the resulting API would look like. It feels weird to demand that ```create_node``` be thread safe but that other methods can be assumed to only run from the main thread. Perhaps we should at least add some check in the other methods to catch future abuses of this (similar to ```check_main_thread()``` in ```worker.py```).
